content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
August 28,
1992
Marlene Garcia
Office of the Speaker
State Assembly
State Capitol,
Room 219
Sacramento,
CA
95814
Re:
Your Request for Confirmation of
Telephone Advice
Our F±le No.
A-92-564
Dear Ms.
Garcia:
This is to confirm telephone advice I provided to you
concerning the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
Specifically,
you indicated you are a legislative employee
who handles higher education and other issues for Speaker Willie
L.
Brown and that you have accepted a teaching position with Los
Rios Community College District which will pay you $1,000 for the
semester.
You asked whether your acceptance of the teaching
position created
a conflict of interest or any other problem under
the Act.
As I stated on the telephone,
you do not have a conflict-of-
interest under the Act because the payment you will receive from
Los Rios Community College District is not considered to be
“income” for conflict—of—interest purposes because the District is
a governmental agency.
(See Sections 82030(b) (2),
87100 and
87103 .)
Furthermore,
your acceptance of the $1,000 for teaching does
not violate the Act’s ban against the acceptance of honoraria in
exchange for making speeches.
(See Section 89503.)
This is
because the Act permits designated employees such as you to accept
payments for speaking as part of a bona fide profession such as
teaching.
(See Sections 89503(b)
and 89502(c).)
Regulation
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
e to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000,
g.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A-92-564
Page
2
18932.2(b)
2 creates a presumption that teaching at an educational
institution accredited or authorized by the State of California is
“bona fide” for purposes of the Act.
One issue raised by your question that is not within the
Commission’s jurisdiction is that of incompatible offices.
You
may wish to contact the Attorney General’s Office to determine if
your acceptance of this teaching position and your work for
Speaker Brown creates any problems in this regard.
Should you have any questions, please contact me at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincer ly,
-
/ Co t Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
SH:aa
2
Adopted by the Commission on January 14,
1992.
Copies of
Commission regulations may be obtained from the Commission at a
charge of 10c per page.

sections.question: You indicated you are a legislative employee who handles higher education and other issues for Speaker Willie L. Brown and that you have accepted a teaching position with Los Rios Community College District which will pay you $1,000 for the semester. You asked whether your acceptance of the teaching position created a conflict of interest or any other problem under the Act.
sections.conclusion: You do not have a conflict-of-interest under the Act because the payment you will receive from Los Rios Community College District is not considered to be 'income' for conflict-of-interest purposes because the District is a governmental agency. Furthermore, your acceptance of the $1,000 for teaching does not violate the Act's ban against the acceptance of honoraria in exchange for making speeches because the Act permits designated employees such as you to accept payments for speaking as part of a bona fide profession such as teaching.
sections.question_synthetic: Does a legislative employee's acceptance of a $1,000 teaching position at a governmental community college create a conflict of interest or violate the Political Reform Act's honoraria ban?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that the teaching position does not create a conflict of interest because the community college district is a governmental agency and thus the payment is not considered 'income' under the Act. Additionally, the teaching payment does not violate the honoraria ban because teaching is a bona fide profession for which designated employees may receive compensation.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a confirmation of telephone advice letter. The document contains clear QUESTION and CONCLUSION sections though not explicitly labeled. The advice covers conflict of interest and honoraria provisions under Government Code Sections 82030, 87100, 87103, and 89503. The Commission notes that incompatible offices analysis requires Attorney General review.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You indicated you are a legislative employee who handles higher education and other issues for Speaker Willie L. Brown and that you have accepted a teaching position with Los Rios Community College District which will pay you $1,000 for the semester. You asked whether your acceptance of the teaching position created a conflict of interest or any other problem under the Act.

CONCLUSION: You do not have a conflict-of-interest under the Act because the payment you will receive from Los Rios Community College District is not considered to be 'income' for conflict-of-interest purposes because the District is a governmental agency. Furthermore, your acceptance of the $1,000 for teaching does not violate the Act's ban against the acceptance of honoraria in exchange for making speeches because the Act permits designated employees such as you to accept payments for speaking as part of a bona fide profession such as teaching.

Brown and that you have accepted a teaching position with Los Rios Community College District which will pay you $1,000 for the semester. You asked whether your acceptance of the teaching position created a conflict of interest or any other problem under the Act. As I stated on the telephone, you do not have a conflict-of- interest under the Act because the payment you will receive from Los Rios Community College District is not considered to be “income” for conflict—of—interest purposes because the District is a governmental agency. (See Sections 82030(b) (2), 87100 and 87103 .) Furthermore, your acceptance of the $1,000 for teaching does not violate the Act’s ban against the acceptance of honoraria in exchange for making speeches. (See Section 89503.) This is because the Act permits designated employees such as you to accept payments for speaking as part of a bona fide profession such as teaching. (See Sections 89503(b) and 89502(c).) One issue raised by your question that is not within the Commission’s jurisdiction is that of incompatible offices. You may wish to contact the Attorney General’s Office to determine if your acceptance of this teaching position and your work for Speaker Brown creates any problems in this regard. embedding.summary: The FPPC confirms telephone advice that a legislative employee may accept a $1,000 teaching position at Los Rios Community College District without creating a conflict of interest or violating the honoraria ban under the Political Reform Act, though the Commission notes that incompatible offices issues fall outside its jurisdiction.
